content.text: React exposes three keyboard event props:
is the right choice for nearly everything. It fires before the character appears, it fires for every key including non-printable ones (arrows, Escape, Tab), and it repeats while a key is held.

attrs.alt: Timeline showing onKeyDown fires first (repeats while key is held), deprecated onKeyPress fires next, then onKeyUp fires once on release. Properties box shows event.key, event.code, event.repeat, modifier keys. Right panel shows common event.key values like Enter, Escape, ArrowUp.
attrs.title: Keyboard Event Lifecycle and Key Properties

content.text: Both events fire on any focused element. The key difference:
— fires before the character is inserted into the input value. You can call
here to block the character entirely. Also fires repeatedly with
— fires once after the key is released. The character is already in the input value. Useful for "done typing" signals where you only want to act once per key press, not once per repeat.
event.key — The Right Property to Check
to identify which key was pressed. Never use the deprecated
— browsers still support them but they are removed from the web platform specification.
The printed character for printable keys:
A named string for special keys:
The modifier itself when you press a modifier alone:
There is a subtle but important distinction between
— what character the key produces given the current keyboard layout. Pressing the physical Q key on a French AZERTY keyboard gives
because that is the character at that position on an AZERTY layout.
— the physical key location, always in US QWERTY terms:
for text-aware logic (Enter to submit, Escape to close). Use
for game controls or shortcuts that should match physical position regardless of layout (WASD movement should work the same on AZERTY where the character keys are in different positions).
Modifier keys (Ctrl, Shift, Alt, Meta/Cmd) are exposed as boolean properties. Check them in combination with
On macOS the Cmd key is
. Always check both for cross-platform shortcuts:
Keyboard events only fire on a focused element. The browser automatically sends keyboard events to whichever element has focus. The rules:

— React's synthetic events don't capture this scenario.
A classic use of keyboard events is a tag input or autocomplete widget where Enter adds a tag, Backspace removes the last tag when the input is empty, and arrow keys navigate suggestions:
Arrow key navigation is a common accessibility requirement (required by ARIA patterns for menus, listboxes, and comboboxes). The pattern is to track an active index in state and move it with
so the page doesn't scroll at the same time:
Input Method Editors (IME) let users type Chinese, Japanese, or Korean characters using multiple keystrokes to compose a single character. During composition,
fires for each keystroke but the value isn't committed yet. If you submit on Enter you may submit mid-composition. Use the
property (on the native event) or the composition events to guard against this:
— deprecated and doesn't fire for non-printable keys (Escape, arrows). Use
— arrow keys scroll the page by default. Call
Attaching to a non-focusable div without tabIndex
— keyboard events will never fire. Add
— triggering submit on Enter during CJK composition will submit before the user finishes typing their character.
for almost everything — it fires first, works for non-printable keys, and lets you call
to identify the key. Never use the deprecated
for position-based shortcuts (game controls) that must work regardless of keyboard layout.
Elements must be focused to receive keyboard events. Add
to make a non-interactive element focusable.
Guard Enter-to-submit with an IME composition check when your app targets CJK locales.
